metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how findings tagged with the new `applies_when` values enter `active_triggers`: either (a) map the new moves to an EXISTING overlay ID that is already emitted as a `criteria` value (e.g. `delete-bad-test` → `applies_when: ["tests"]`, relying on the LLM to pick the move only for change-detector findings), or (b) document how a reviewer prompt is updated to emit the new tag as a `criteria` value so it propagates into `active_triggers`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new `applies_when` tag values (e.g. `"pre-existing"` / `"change-detector"`) for the two new moves, but `active_triggers` in `workflow_ops.py` are built exclusively from the `criteria` fields of surviving findings. Those `criteria` values are emitted by overlay reviewers whose prompts constrain them to the closed `OVERLAY_IDS` vocabulary. If no reviewer emits a finding with `criteria` containing the new tag, `active_triggers` will never include it, so the applicability filter will never surface these moves — they register but never fire.
findings.location: What / applies_when gating section; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py line 317; src/rebar/llm/code_review/moves.py
findings.suggested_fix: For `delete-bad-test`, use `applies_when: ["tests"]` since change-detector findings already arrive tagged with the `tests` overlay. For `file-follow-on-ticket`, either use `applies_when: []` (always-applicable, LLM picks when appropriate) or identify which existing overlay the pre-existing-issue finding arrives under and key on that.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the tag values chosen for the new moves' `applies_when` fields will actually appear in `active_triggers` (derived from `finding.criteria[]` which only carries OVERLAY_IDS values); either map delete-bad-test to the existing `'tests'` tag and file-follow-on-ticket to a suitable existing OVERLAY_ID, or extend the active_triggers derivation to also carry finding-level sub-tags if novel tags are required.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that applies_when can be extended with new tag values ('pre-existing/out-of-scope', 'change-detector/tautological') to gate the two new moves, but active_triggers are populated exclusively from finding.criteria[], whose values are drawn from the closed OVERLAY_IDS tuple. A tag NOT in OVERLAY_IDS will never appear in active_triggers, making any move with applies_when: ['change-detector'] (or any other novel tag) permanently non-applicable via the trigger mechanism — the move would never fire.
findings.suggested_fix: For delete-bad-test: use `applies_when: ['tests']` (change-detector findings are emitted by the 'tests' overlay and carry `criteria: ['tests']`). For file-follow-on-ticket: use `applies_when: ['always']` (pre-existing issues are orthogonal to overlay — any overlay can surface one) or pick the overlay whose finding type most commonly flags pre-existing issues. If a sub-tag scheme is genuinely needed, the active_triggers computation in workflow_ops.py must be extended to include it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the end-to-end plumbing for the new 'pre-existing' tag: how does a finding get `criteria` containing this tag (e.g. does the base/overlay reviewer emit it, or does a detector inject it?), and is a routing entry needed for it to pass through the criteria-validation layer?
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `file-follow-on-ticket` will key on a new 'pre-existing/out-of-scope' tag in `applies_when`, but the coach mechanism's `active_triggers` is populated from the `criteria` fields of surviving findings — which the code-review system expects to match the closed overlay/criterion ID vocabulary. The path by which a 'pre-existing' tag appears in a finding's `criteria` (and thus in `active_triggers`) is unspecified, making this an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: What section: 'applies_when gating' / Acceptance Criteria AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define how the new applies_when tag values ('pre-existing', 'change-detector', or whatever they are named) enter active_triggers — either by enriching finding output schemas with a tags[] field, or by using always-applicable moves (no applies_when), or by a separate trigger-injection mechanism — and document this protocol co-located with the move definitions.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the new applies_when tag values reach active_triggers at coach-invocation time. The live workflow derives active_triggers exclusively from findings' criteria[] fields (criterion IDs like 'E5', 'T5a'), not from any free-form tagging field. A finding tagged as a 'pre-existing issue' or 'change-detector' carries no mechanism to inject a string like 'pre-existing' into active_triggers, so the new moves would never be selected.
findings.location: What section / the applies_when gating description; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:317

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concretely how the new applies_when tags (e.g. 'pre-existing', 'change-detector') will be registered without polluting OVERLAY_IDS (which has a separate escalation-target semantic) and without breaking the test_move_catalog_validates_and_uses_closed_applies_when_vocabulary gate at test_code_review_ws2.py:78 — e.g. widen the allowed-tag set to `OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {'always'} ∪ FINDING_CATEGORY_TAGS` and update the enforcement test accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan proposes extending the applies_when tag vocabulary with new values (e.g. a 'pre-existing/out-of-scope' tag for file-follow-on-ticket), but the existing enforcement test gates applies_when tags strictly to `set(reg.OVERLAY_IDS) | {"always"}`. OVERLAY_IDS has a distinct semantic meaning (base-reviewer escalation targets, not finding categories), so adding 'pre-existing' or 'change-detector' to OVERLAY_IDS would be a category error. The plan does not specify whether it will extend OVERLAY_IDS (wrong), broaden the enforcement test to a larger tag namespace, or introduce a second parallel tag set — creating a risk of either polluting the closed overlay-id vocab or breaking the existing test gate.
findings.location: What section: 'applies_when gating (specified — the tag vocabulary is closed, so define the values these key on)'; AC line 2; src/rebar/llm/code_review/moves.py applies_when vocabulary; tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py:78
findings.suggested_fix: Define a separate constant (e.g. `FINDING_CATEGORY_TAGS = frozenset({'pre-existing', 'change-detector'})`) in moves.py, and update the enforcement test's `allowed` set to `set(reg.OVERLAY_IDS) | {'always'} | moves.FINDING_CATEGORY_TAGS`. This keeps OVERLAY_IDS semantically clean while extending the tag vocabulary in a tracked, testable way.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact string values for the two new applies_when tags and specify where they are registered (e.g. a new `MOVE_TRIGGER_IDS` constant separate from `OVERLAY_IDS`), updating the enforcement test's `allowed` set accordingly.
findings.finding: The new applies_when tag string values for file-follow-on-ticket and delete-bad-test are never defined anywhere in the plan, and the existing enforcement test (test_move_catalog_validates_and_uses_closed_applies_when_vocabulary) rejects any applies_when tag not in `set(reg.OVERLAY_IDS) | {'always'}` — so new tags would fail load-time validation without a named vocabulary extension point.
findings.location: What section / AC2: 'applies_when tag values each move keys on are defined explicitly (vocabulary extended if needed)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a separate `MOVE_TRIGGER_IDS: tuple[str, ...] = ('pre-existing', 'change-detector')` (or equivalent) in `moves.py` or `registry.py`, change the enforcement test's `allowed` to `set(reg.OVERLAY_IDS) | set(moves.MOVE_TRIGGER_IDS) | {'always'}`, and name those exact string values in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where new `applies_when` tag values (e.g. `pre-existing`, `change-detector`) enter the `active_triggers` pipeline — either name `registry.py` (`OVERLAY_IDS`) as an edit target with its blast-radius enumerated, or define an alternative tag-injection mechanism that doesn't touch `OVERLAY_IDS`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies extending the `applies_when` tag vocabulary but does not name `registry.py` (where `OVERLAY_IDS` is defined) as an edit target, even though `active_triggers` is sourced from findings' `criteria` fields, which flow from `OVERLAY_IDS`-validated overlay IDs. If new tags (e.g. `pre-existing`, `change-detector`) need to appear in findings' `criteria` to fire the moves, they must either be added to `OVERLAY_IDS` in `registry.py` or the plan must specify an alternative mechanism — but registry.py is absent from the stated scope.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## What — 'Extend the applies_when tag vocabulary'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If the vocabulary extension touches `OVERLAY_IDS` in `registry.py`, enumerate and address the consumers outside `moves.py`: the overlay dispatch loop in `workflow_ops.py`, `overlay_id_enum()` (consumed by the base-reviewer prompt), and `filter_recommend_overlays` — or confirm new tags bypass `OVERLAY_IDS` entirely.
findings.finding: The plan names `registry.py`'s `OVERLAY_IDS` mechanism indirectly (via 'tag vocabulary is closed') but does not enumerate the callers OUTSIDE `code_review/` that would be affected if new overlay IDs are added to `OVERLAY_IDS`: the overlay dispatch loop (`workflow_ops.py` line 87), the base-reviewer prompt (which enumerates `overlay_id_enum()`), and `filter_recommend_overlays` in `registry.py` — all of which are outside `moves.py`.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/moves.py and the coach template set; no verdict-path change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) that explicitly verifies the `file-follow-on-ticket` template instructs users to set the `discovered_from` field, so the rebar integration is testable.
findings.finding: The What section specifies that the `file-follow-on-ticket` template must include 'guidance to create a discovered_from ticket', but no AC verifies this specific content requirement — AC1 only checks that 'deterministic templates exist', not that they contain the discovered_from linkage guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 vs. What section (file-follow-on-ticket description)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 or add AC4: 'The file-follow-on-ticket template's guidance explicitly references the discovered_from field (e.g., instructs the user to populate discovered_from on the new ticket).'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide at minimum a skeleton template (with named interpolation slots) for each move, or cite an existing template as the structural reference, so 'deterministic templates' in AC1 is concretely verifiable.
findings.finding: The template content for both moves is unspecified; 'deterministic templates' in AC1 and the What section only says the LLM picks the move and names the subject, but provides no template text, variable list, or structural constraints — an executor must invent the template wording, which is a design decision.
findings.location: What section ('Deterministic templates; the LLM only picks the move and names the subject') and AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan (e.g. parametrized unit tests in tests/rebar/llm/code_review/) that asserts: (a) a finding tagged change-detector/tautological selects delete-bad-test and not any 'improve' move, (b) a pre-existing/out-of-scope finding selects file-follow-on-ticket, and (c) subject validation passes/fails as expected.
findings.finding: The plan introduces conditional applies_when routing logic and move-selection behavior (change-detector → delete-bad-test, not 'improve the test') but includes no testing plan — no mention of unit tests, integration tests, or fixture-based validation of the tag-gating logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Tests` section listing at least: `test_delete_bad_test_selected_for_change_detector_finding`, `test_file_follow_on_ticket_selected_for_preexisting_finding`, and `test_applies_when_tag_vocabulary_rejects_unknown_tags` as parametrized pytest cases covering the new conditional logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how AC3's mutual-exclusivity is enforced: either (a) give change-detector findings a distinct sub-tag (e.g. `change-detector`) that `delete-bad-test` keys on while removing `always` from `add-regression-test`'s applies_when, or (b) document that `add-regression-test` will be narrowed to exclude the new sub-tag, so it cannot co-fire.
findings.finding: AC3 ('A change-detector-test finding maps to delete-bad-test, NOT an improve-the-test move') is unachievable under the current additive `applicable_moves()` mechanism: the existing `add-regression-test` move has `applies_when: ["tests", "always"]` and will ALSO be offered whenever `delete-bad-test` fires on a `tests`-tagged finding. The plan does not specify how this mutual-exclusivity requirement is satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `"always"` from `add-regression-test`'s `applies_when` and add a new `change-detector` tag to the vocabulary; give `delete-bad-test` `applies_when: ["change-detector"]` and `add-regression-test` `applies_when: ["tests"]` (without `always` and without `change-detector`). A change-detector finding then carries `criteria: ["change-detector"]`, which fires only `delete-bad-test`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'subject validation' explicitly in AC2: state the rules (e.g. non-empty string, no whitespace-only, max length, allowed characters) so the criterion is observable and self-contained.
findings.finding: 'pass subject validation' in AC2 references validation rules that are never defined anywhere in the plan; an executing agent cannot know what constitutes a passing vs. failing subject without stopping to ask or inventing the rules.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: '...and pass subject validation'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pass subject validation' with the actual invariants, e.g.: 'subject is a non-empty string identifying the affected symbol/file; validated by the existing MoveSubject schema (or a newly defined one — specify field types and constraints here).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC, e.g. a pytest invocation that exercises the routing logic (AC3: a change-detector-tagged finding routes to delete-bad-test) and the move registration (AC1/AC2).
findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any of the three ACs; there is no test invocation, CLI command, or grep that would produce evidence of completion — all three ACs are stated as structural facts with no executable verification path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each AC a parenthetical proving command, e.g. AC3: '(proved by: pytest tests/test_moves.py::test_change_detector_routes_to_delete_bad_test)'; AC1/AC2: '(proved by: pytest tests/test_moves.py::test_move_registration_and_subject_validation)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'subject validation' in AC2: specify what the validation rules are (e.g., format constraints, allowed values, where enforced) so the criterion is end-state-observable.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'pass subject validation' but the plan never defines what subject validation is or how it will be verified, leaving the end-state-observable proof for that sub-criterion incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the plan for why tag-gated `applies_when` is chosen over always-applicable for these two moves (e.g. 'to prevent the moves firing on unrelated findings' or 'consistent with the threat-model / add-migration-guard precedent in code_review/moves.py').
findings.finding: The plan states the approach (deterministic templates, tag-gated applicability) but gives no positive rationale for WHY tag-gated applies_when is chosen over always-applicable moves — the distinction is load-bearing because it determines whether the moves ever fire, yet the 'Why' section only explains the DSO lessons, not the design choice.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
